title: Coffee on Silk
timeEstimate: Pro-lane within hours; home is damage-prevention only
stainChemistry: Coffee leaves a brown stain made of tannins plus the browning compounds from the roast. Coffee with milk adds milk protein and butterfat — and the protein sets fast under heat. Silk is fragile across the board: enzymes that lift milk protein and stains from cotton would damage silk's own protein, hot water sets the stain and weakens the fiber, and most household stain removers (OxiClean, peroxide, chlorine bleach) damage silk. The honest path on coffee plus silk is cold blot at home and a pro within hours.
whyThisWorks: Cold water flushes fresh coffee out before it locks in. A specialist can then use cold detergent plus a controlled mild acid plus a silk-safe reducing bleach to clear what's left without harming the fiber. Home chemistry has too many ways to make this worse on silk — vinegar over-dwell weakens it, peroxide yellows it, hot water sets the stain. Home protocol stays minimal: cold blot, no chemistry escalation, pro fast.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): coffee browning is melanoidins from Maillard reactions plus chlorogenic acid plus tannin polyphenols. Tannins anchor to silk fibroin via hydrogen bonding. Sodium hydrosulfite reduces the chromophore at controlled dilution; acetic acid breaks the tannin-fibroin bonds without weakening silk when dwell is held under 60 seconds.
defaultAssumption: Silk + coffee = pro within hours. Home is damage-prevention before pro sees it. Black coffee is slightly easier than coffee-with-cream (no protein); both are pro-lane on silk.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water through the BACK over a white catch towel. Press, no scrubbing. Most fresh coffee tannin transfers in 60–90 seconds when caught immediately.
Diluted acetic acid via dropper on residual stain. Press cloth — no rubbing. STRICT 60-sec ceiling on silk. Flush cold immediately.
For oxidized brown only. Reducing bleach is silk-safe at controlled dilution. Strict 60-sec dwell, then thorough cold rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, cold water, dry white towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD flush to remove all residue. Cold water final rinse until clear. Press between dry towels, lay flat to air-dry, never hang.

steps: 1. Cold water back-flush at first contact.
3. Sodium hydrosulfite for oxidized residue, 60-sec ceiling.
warnings: NEVER hot water — denatures silk + sets tannin
NEVER enzymes — Protein digests silk fibroin
NEVER OxiClean / alkaline cleaners — weakens silk
NEVER hydrogen peroxide on silk — yellows dyes

homeSolutions.instruction: Coffee on silk is a pro-lane case. The tools that work on cotton (OxiClean, hot water, peroxide) all damage silk. Even gentle home chemistry risks more than it lifts. Your home job is exactly two things: cold-water back-flush to extract bulk coffee, and get to a silk specialist within hours. The garment's best outcome is the one where you bring it in with nothing else applied.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Back-Flush — Pour Through, Not On Top
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn inside out. Place a white towel under the stain. Run COLD water (ice cold preferred) through the BACK of the stain into the catch towel. Cold only — heat denatures silk and sets coffee tannin. The water runs THROUGH the silk, not pooled on top. 60 seconds of patient flushing usually pulls fresh coffee to a pale shadow.

homeSolutions.agent: Press Dry — Don't Apply Anything Else
homeSolutions.instruction: Press silk between two dry white towels to lift moisture. No wringing, no twisting, no hanging wet. Lay flat at room temperature, out of sun. Do NOT apply OxiClean, peroxide, vinegar, baking soda, or any other home cleaner. The silk's best outcome is reaching a pro with nothing but a cold-water rinse already attempted.

CALL FIRST — "Do you treat silk with acetic acid + sodium hydrosulfite for tannin?" If they don't understand the question, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Coffee on silk, age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied. [Black coffee / with milk]." The milk-vs-black detail matters — adds protein to the chemistry plan.

steps: 2. Cold water back-flush from the reverse, into a catch towel.
3. Press dry between white towels — no other chemistry.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop after the cold-water back-flush. Anything else home-applied risks worse damage than the coffee.
warnings: NEVER scrub or wring wet silk
Pour through, not on top — water rings are as visible as coffee

neverDo: Never wring or scrub wet silk
fiberSensitivities: Silk is protein fiber — same enzyme-digestion risk as wool/cashmere

materialWarnings: Pro within hours — home is damage-prevention only
Cold back-flush is the entire home protocol
No vinegar, no peroxide, no OxiClean at home

escalation.when: Always — silk + coffee is pro-lane, regardless of stain age.
escalation.whatToTell: "Coffee on silk, [black / with milk], age [hours], cold-water back-flush ONLY at home. No acid, no peroxide, no detergent applied."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in silk (call ahead — confirm acetic acid + sodium hydrosulfite spotting)

contextQuestions: Black coffee or coffee with milk/cream?
How long ago — minutes, hours, or older?
Type of silk — charmeuse / chiffon / habotai / dupioni?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Coffee with cream adds protein — pro plans differently (cold approach + protein step before tannin). Thinner silks (chiffon, habotai) are more vulnerable. Anything pre-applied at home — especially vinegar, peroxide, or OxiClean — disclose honestly; changes what's still possible.
